food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dish with grilled meat and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694) but no specific entry for nasi goreng with chicken satay. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category. No specific nasi goreng with satay composite code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for nasi goreng with chicken satay as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi goreng with chicken satay as typically served in a Singaporean hawker centre or Indonesian restaurant, comprising approximately 250 g fried rice and 200 g chicken satay with peanut sauce and fresh garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice) and Satay Ayam (Chicken Satay) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for nasi goreng (plain fried rice) and chicken satay separately. Values for this composite dish are derived by proportional combination: approximately 56% fried rice (250 g) and 44% chicken satay with peanut sauce (200 g) per standard 450 g serving, then normalised to per-100 g. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used to cross-check individual ingredient macros. This is an adapted composite estimate; exact restaurant formulation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice / nasi goreng analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi goreng with chicken satay as a composite dish. GI of 55 is derived as a weighted estimate: nasi goreng (plain fried rice) has a reported GI of approximately 56–60 in the University of Sydney GI Database (analogous to cooked white rice stir-fried with egg and soy sauce); chicken satay and peanut sauce contribute negligible glycemic load due to high protein and fat content. Weighted composite GI estimated at 55 (low). Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (55 × 105.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 58.1. Available carbohydrate per serving = 23.5 g/100 g × 450 g = 105.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~2160 mg per 450 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce, peanut sauce, and seasoning, which can contribute over 90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg in a single serving; request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~58 GL units) driven by refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as the large portion of refined white rice produces a high glycemic load of approximately 58 per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control, substituting brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is recommended to moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium; peanut sauce adds unsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; while the peanut sauce contributes heart-neutral monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats, the overall sodium content is high and the dish is energy-dense, and limiting portion size to half a plate and requesting less sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng with Chicken Satay is a popular Singaporean-Indonesian composite dish combining two iconic Southeast Asian preparations. Nasi goreng (literally 'fried rice' in Malay and Indonesian) is wok-fried cooked rice stir-fried at high heat with egg, shallots, garlic, chilli, and dark and light soy sauce, producing a smoky, savoury, slightly spicy rice with a characteristic wok-hei aroma. The dish is served alongside sate ayam — chicken satay — consisting of marinated chicken pieces threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal or a gas flame, seasoned with lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, and coriander. The satay is accompanied by a rich, slightly sweet peanut sauce (kuah kacang) made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, palm sugar, and spices, along with fresh cucumber slices and compressed rice cakes (ketupat or lontong). The dish is energy-dense, providing approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with a macronutrient profile of roughly 23.5 g carbohydrate, 9.8 g protein, and 7.2 g fat per 100 g. A standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 878 kcal. The sodium content is high (~480 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load per serving is high (~58 GL units) owing to the refined white rice base, which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is widely available in Singaporean hawker centres, Indonesian restaurants, and food delivery platforms, and is considered a comfort food with strong cultural significance across Singapore, Indonesia, and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: No single HPB Foodo or approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish (nasi goreng with chicken satay as a combined plate). Values are derived by proportional combination of HPB Foodo nasi goreng and chicken satay entries, normalised to per-100 g, and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach and variability in restaurant formulations.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from HPB Foodo nasi goreng and chicken satay entries combined in a 250 g : 200 g ratio (fried rice : satay with peanut sauce) and normalised to per-100 g. Actual values will vary by restaurant and preparation. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite dish. 4. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on minor contributions from cooking oils; this is a trace estimate. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for fried rice / nasi goreng; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 6. The dish is classified as halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and soy sauce, as is standard in Singapore hawker contexts. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy peanut sauce and chicken data; not directly reported in HPB Foodo. 8. Nasi Goreng Ayam Sate appears in aliases_by_language indonesian and is included in the flat aliases list.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full composite nutrient profile from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. Added all mandatory multilingual names for Singaporean cuisine. Populated GI as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Added ontology with closest_parent codes and gap notes. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) in notes.

Added 'Nasi Goreng Ayam Sate' to the flat `aliases` list to ensure consistency with `aliases_by_language.indonesian`. Confirmed zero-macro justification not needed as all core macros are non-zero. Confirmed GI > 0 (55). Confirmed all -1 values documented in notes. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass.
